subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: No FLA Property Equalization for Unmarried Partners
fact_pattern: Jessica and Mark have cohabited for 9 years without marrying. They have one child together. They now separate. Jessica contributed significantly to the home (registered solely in Mark's name) by paying renovation costs and acting as the primary caregiver for their child. Jessica asks whether she is entitled to property equalization under the Family Law Act.
call_of_question: Is Jessica entitled to property equalization under Part I of the Family Law Act as a common-law spouse?

options.A: Yes — after 3 years of cohabitation, unmarried spouses acquire the same property equalization rights as married spouses under the FLA.
options.B: Yes — any person who shares a child with another person is entitled to FLA property equalization regardless of whether they were married.
options.C: No — Part I of the Family Law Act (equalization of net family property) applies only to married spouses; Jessica and Mark are unmarried and Jessica has no right to equalization of net family property under the FLA.
options.D: Yes — after 5 years of cohabitation, a common-law spouse is automatically deemed a 'spouse' under all provisions of the FLA, including property equalization.

explanation: Part I of the Family Law Act (equalization of net family property, including the matrimonial home regime) applies exclusively to married spouses. Section 1(1) of the FLA defines 'spouse' for the purposes of Part I to mean only persons who are or were married to each other. Common-law partners — regardless of the length of cohabitation or whether they have children together — have no right to equalization of net family property under the FLA. Jessica must rely on other legal theories (such as unjust enrichment or resulting trust) to assert a claim to an interest in the home.
why_A_wrong: The FLA does not grant property equalization rights to unmarried cohabiting spouses, regardless of the length of cohabitation. The three-year cohabitation threshold in the FLA relates only to support rights (s.29), not property rights.
why_B_wrong: Sharing a child does not confer FLA property equalization rights. The FLA Part I equalization regime applies solely to married spouses. Parenthood is relevant to spousal support (FLA s.29) but not to property equalization.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA Part I property equalization applies only to married spouses; Jessica as a common-law partner has no FLA equalization rights and must pursue other legal remedies.
why_D_wrong: No five-year cohabitation period converts a common-law relationship into a marriage for FLA property equalization purposes. The FLA does not deem cohabiting partners to be 'spouses' for property equalization purposes regardless of cohabitation length.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: No FLA Property Equalization for Unmarried Partners
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 1(1) (definition of spouse — Part I), Part I

tested_concepts: FLA Part I applies only to married spouses

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Unjust Enrichment as Basis for Property Claim by Unmarried Partners
fact_pattern: After 12 years of cohabitation, Sandra and Greg separate. During the relationship, Sandra worked part-time and managed the home and their two children, allowing Greg to advance his career. Greg accumulated significant savings and a business. The home is registered in Greg's name. Sandra now claims an interest in Greg's property.
call_of_question: What is the primary legal basis on which a common-law partner may claim an interest in property held in the other partner's name upon relationship breakdown in Ontario?

options.A: The Family Law Act — Part I equalization applies equally to all couples who have cohabited for 12 or more years.
options.B: Adverse possession — Sandra may claim property through long-term occupation.
options.C: Unjust enrichment — where one partner has been enriched at the expense of the other, without juristic reason, in circumstances where a corresponding deprivation has occurred; the court may remedy the unjust enrichment by imposing a constructive trust or awarding a monetary remedy.
options.D: Statutory resulting trust — Ontario family law creates an automatic resulting trust in favour of any long-term cohabiting partner.

explanation: In the absence of FLA equalization rights, a common-law partner who claims an interest in property held by the other partner must rely on the equitable doctrine of unjust enrichment as developed in Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834 and refined in Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269. Unjust enrichment requires proof of: (1) enrichment of the defendant; (2) a corresponding deprivation of the plaintiff; and (3) the absence of a juristic reason for the enrichment. If established, the court may impose a constructive trust over specific property or award a monetary remedy proportionate to the contribution.
why_A_wrong: FLA Part I equalization does not apply to common-law partners regardless of the length of cohabitation. Twelve years of cohabitation does not trigger FLA property equalization rights.
why_B_wrong: Adverse possession requires hostile, open, and continuous possession inconsistent with the true owner's title — it does not apply to domestic partners sharing a home in a consensual relationship.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Unjust enrichment (Pettkus v. Becker; Kerr v. Baranow) is the primary legal doctrine for a common-law partner's property claim upon separation; remedied by constructive trust or monetary award.
why_D_wrong: There is no statutory resulting trust created automatically for cohabiting partners in Ontario family law. Resulting trusts may arise in specific circumstances (e.g., gratuitous transfer) but are not an automatic entitlement for long-term common-law partners.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Unjust Enrichment — Basis for Common-Law Property Claim
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834; Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3

tested_concepts: three elements enrichment deprivation no juristic reason

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Elements of Unjust Enrichment — Enrichment, Deprivation, No Juristic Reason
fact_pattern: For 10 years, Patricia worked full-time and contributed 40% of her salary to the household expenses, while her common-law partner Derek paid the mortgage on his home (registered in his name alone) and invested in his business. Patricia did not do more than her fair share of household work; she paid equally for shared expenses. She now claims unjust enrichment and seeks a constructive trust over Derek's home.
call_of_question: Is Patricia likely to establish unjust enrichment entitling her to a constructive trust over Derek's home?

options.A: Yes — any contribution to household expenses by a cohabiting partner automatically establishes unjust enrichment and entitles the contributor to a constructive trust.
options.B: No — only contributions in the form of unpaid domestic labour (not monetary contributions to shared expenses) can ground an unjust enrichment claim.
options.C: Likely no — while Patricia made monetary contributions, a 50% equal sharing of household expenses does not give rise to unjust enrichment; Derek received no enrichment beyond what was bargained for in the mutual support of the household, and there is likely a juristic reason (equal contribution arrangement) negating the claim.
options.D: Yes — cohabitation for 10 years automatically establishes unjust enrichment entitling the claimant to a 50% interest in all jointly used property.

explanation: Unjust enrichment requires: (1) enrichment of the defendant; (2) a corresponding deprivation of the plaintiff; and (3) no juristic reason for the enrichment (Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269). Where Patricia contributed equally to shared household expenses (a mutual arrangement), Derek received no unjust benefit — he paid his share and Patricia paid hers. The equal contribution arrangement constitutes a juristic reason (a mutual benefit-conferring arrangement) that negates the unjust enrichment claim. Patricia's failure to show that she contributed disproportionately — beyond what was expected in a mutual sharing arrangement — makes it unlikely that the third element (no juristic reason) is met.
why_A_wrong: Paying a proportionate share of household expenses does not automatically establish unjust enrichment. The court must assess whether there is an enrichment of the defendant beyond what was agreed upon and whether there is no juristic reason for the enrichment.
why_B_wrong: Unjust enrichment can be established on the basis of monetary contributions as well as domestic labour; the form of the contribution is not limited to unpaid domestic work. However, the quality and disproportionality of the contribution matter.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Equal contribution to shared household expenses likely constitutes a juristic reason (mutual benefit arrangement), negating the third element of unjust enrichment; the claim is unlikely to succeed on these facts.
why_D_wrong: Length of cohabitation does not automatically establish unjust enrichment or a proportionate interest in property. The unjust enrichment analysis is fact-specific, not based on time alone.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Unjust Enrichment — Three Elements
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Joint Family Venture — Kerr v. Baranow Four Factors
fact_pattern: After a 15-year cohabitation during which Rose and her partner Henry jointly built a business, raised children, and pooled their incomes into a joint account, they separated. Henry's business had grown substantially in value. Rose claims a proportionate share of the business based on the concept of joint family venture. Henry argues that unjust enrichment alone governs and Rose's claim must be limited to what she can trace.
call_of_question: What is the 'joint family venture' concept articulated in Kerr v. Baranow, and how does it affect the remedy for unjust enrichment?

options.A: Joint family venture is a statutory concept under the FLA that applies to common-law couples after 10 years of cohabitation.
options.B: Joint family venture is not a recognized legal concept; unjust enrichment is limited to a monetary quantum meruit remedy calculated on an hourly wage basis.
options.C: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court recognized that where the parties engaged in a joint family venture — assessed by mutual effort, economic integration, actual intent, and priority of the family — the appropriate remedy for unjust enrichment may be a proportionate share of the accumulated wealth, rather than a strict value-survived or quantum meruit calculation.
options.D: Joint family venture creates an automatic 50/50 split of all assets accumulated during cohabitation, similar to matrimonial home rules.

explanation: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court of Canada articulated the concept of 'joint family venture' as a means of determining the appropriate remedy for unjust enrichment claims between common-law partners. Where the parties' relationship demonstrates: (1) mutual effort; (2) economic integration; (3) actual intent; and (4) priority of the family — the court may award a proportionate share of the wealth accumulated through the joint effort, rather than merely a quantum meruit (hourly wage equivalent) or strict tracing of contributions. This approach is more generous and reflects the reality of long-term cohabiting relationships that function economically like marriages.
why_A_wrong: Joint family venture is a judicial doctrine developed by the Supreme Court of Canada in Kerr v. Baranow; it is not a statutory concept under the FLA and has no duration threshold.
why_B_wrong: The Supreme Court in Kerr v. Baranow rejected the view that unjust enrichment remedies for cohabiting partners are always limited to quantum meruit. The joint family venture approach may entitle the claimant to a proportionate share of the total family wealth accumulated through mutual effort.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Kerr v. Baranow established the joint family venture doctrine assessed through four factors (mutual effort, economic integration, actual intent, priority of family), enabling a proportionate share remedy for unjust enrichment.
why_D_wrong: Joint family venture does not create an automatic 50/50 split. The remedy is proportionate to the claimant's actual contribution to the joint effort, which may be more or less than 50% depending on the circumstances.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Joint Family Venture — Kerr v. Baranow
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Spousal Support — FLA s.29 Definition of Spouse for Support Purposes
fact_pattern: Carla and Howard cohabited for 2.5 years in a conjugal relationship and separated without having any children together. Carla now seeks spousal support from Howard under the Family Law Act. Howard argues that she is not his 'spouse' for support purposes because they never married and cohabited for less than three years.
call_of_question: Under the Family Law Act s.29, is Carla entitled to seek spousal support as a common-law partner?

options.A: Yes — any person who has cohabited with another in a conjugal relationship for any period of time may seek spousal support under the FLA.
options.B: No — Howard is correct; under FLA s.29, a common-law partner must have cohabited continuously for at least three years or be the parent of a child with the other person; Carla has cohabited for only 2.5 years and they have no children together, so she does not qualify as a 'spouse' for support purposes.
options.C: Yes — because Carla is a common-law partner, she automatically qualifies as a 'spouse' under all provisions of the FLA including support.
options.D: No — common-law partners can never claim spousal support under the Family Law Act; they must proceed under the Divorce Act.

explanation: Family Law Act s.29 defines 'spouse' for the purposes of Part III (support) to include a person who has cohabited with another person continuously for a period of not less than three years, OR a person who is in a relationship of some permanence if they are the natural or adoptive parents of a child together. Carla cohabited for only 2.5 years and has no children with Howard — neither threshold in s.29 is met. Howard's argument is correct: Carla does not qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 and cannot claim spousal support under the FLA. (Note: The Divorce Act applies only to married persons and is unavailable to Carla.)
why_A_wrong: FLA s.29 requires a minimum of three continuous years of cohabitation, or a relationship with a child. There is no entitlement to spousal support from any period of cohabitation regardless of length.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.29 requires three years of continuous cohabitation or parenthood of a child together; 2.5 years of cohabitation with no children falls below both thresholds.
why_C_wrong: Being a common-law partner does not automatically qualify a person as a 'spouse' under all FLA provisions. The FLA defines 'spouse' differently for different parts; for support (Part III), the threshold is three years or parenthood of a child.
why_D_wrong: The Divorce Act applies exclusively to married spouses. It is not available to common-law partners who cannot divorce because they were never married. Common-law support rights are governed by provincial legislation (FLA s.29) or the common law.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Spousal Support for Common-Law Partners — FLA s.29
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29 (definition of spouse for support purposes)

tested_concepts: FLA s.29 definition spouse for support

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Unjust Enrichment — Three Elements and Juristic Reason Categories
fact_pattern: After a 14-year common-law relationship, Nadia and Vincent separate. During the relationship, Nadia quit her job to look after their three children and Vincent's parents while Vincent built his contracting business from scratch. The home is registered in Vincent's name. Nadia claims unjust enrichment. Vincent argues he paid Nadia a weekly allowance of $200 for her household work, constituting a juristic reason for retaining the benefit.
call_of_question: Under the unjust enrichment doctrine as articulated in Kerr v. Baranow, does the payment of a modest allowance constitute a juristic reason negating Nadia's claim?

options.A: Any payment to the claimant, however modest, constitutes a complete juristic reason for the defendant's retention of the benefit.
options.B: Under Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the juristic reasons category includes: existing contracts, disposition of law, donative intent, and other policy-based reasons; a nominal $200/week payment may not fully reflect the value of Nadia's contributions and would not constitute a complete juristic reason if the payment was grossly disproportionate to the benefit conferred; the court assesses the reasonableness of the juristic reason and the degree to which it corresponds to the enrichment.
options.C: The juristic reason analysis is not applicable to common-law relationships; unjust enrichment is assessed purely on the value of the claimant's labour.
options.D: A modest payment always provides a complete juristic reason because parties are free to contract on any terms they choose.

explanation: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court confirmed that the absence of a juristic reason is an essential element of unjust enrichment. Juristic reasons include: (1) a contract; (2) a disposition of law; (3) donative intent (the claimant intended to gift the benefit); and (4) other policy reasons. A nominal payment of $200/week as an 'allowance' for extensive child-rearing and household management over 14 years may not constitute an adequate juristic reason if it is grossly disproportionate to the value of the services rendered. The court would assess whether the allowance corresponds meaningfully to the enrichment conferred. Where a payment captures only a fraction of the economic value of the contributions, it does not negate the unjust enrichment claim in full — the claim may succeed for the excess enrichment beyond the payment.
why_A_wrong: A payment does not automatically constitute a complete juristic reason. The court assesses whether the payment genuinely corresponds to the value of the benefit received. A nominal payment does not extinguish a claim for much larger contributions.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Kerr v. Baranow, a juristic reason must adequately account for the enrichment; a nominal $200/week payment for 14 years of unpaid domestic work and child-rearing is likely insufficient to negate the full claim.
why_C_wrong: The juristic reason analysis is directly applicable to common-law relationship claims. Kerr v. Baranow itself arose from a common-law relationship; the unjust enrichment framework with the three-element test applies fully.
why_D_wrong: Freedom of contract does not mean any payment provides a complete juristic reason. The courts evaluate the proportionality of the payment to the benefit conferred. A grossly inadequate payment does not extinguish a larger unjust enrichment claim.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Unjust Enrichment — Juristic Reason Analysis
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834

tested_concepts: Kerr v Baranow juristic reason categories

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Joint Family Venture — Applying the Four Kerr v. Baranow Factors
fact_pattern: Angela and Robert cohabited for 18 years. Throughout the relationship, they each worked full-time, pooled all income into a joint account, made joint decisions about property purchases, cared for their two children together, and treated their financial lives as fully merged. Robert's investments grew significantly. On separation, Angela claims a proportionate share of all accumulated wealth.
call_of_question: Under Kerr v. Baranow, which of the four joint family venture factors are most clearly present on these facts, and what remedy does a finding of joint family venture support?

options.A: None of the four joint family venture factors are present because Angela and Robert both worked; the JFV doctrine only applies where one party worked exclusively in the home.
options.B: Under Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the four JFV factors are: (1) mutual effort; (2) economic integration; (3) actual intent; and (4) priority of the family; all four appear strongly present on these facts (pooled income, joint decisions, children, mutual full commitment); a finding of JFV supports a proportionate share remedy rather than a quantum meruit calculation.
options.C: The joint family venture doctrine entitles Angela to exactly 50% of all assets regardless of her actual contributions.
options.D: Joint family venture applies only where both parties have signed a formal partnership agreement.

explanation: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court identified four factors indicating a joint family venture: (1) mutual effort — working together toward common goals; (2) economic integration — pooling resources and finances; (3) actual intent — the parties' own understanding of their relationship as a joint enterprise; and (4) priority of the family — decisions made in the interests of the family unit. On the facts, all four factors are strongly present: 18 years of cohabitation, pooled income, joint property decisions, raising children together, and fully merged financial lives. A finding of JFV supports a proportionate share remedy for the unjust enrichment claim — Angela would receive a share proportional to her actual contribution to the jointly acquired wealth, which may be close to 50% given the full economic integration.
why_A_wrong: The JFV doctrine is not limited to situations where one party worked solely at home. The Supreme Court in Kerr v. Baranow applied the doctrine to relationships where both parties worked and pooled resources. The relevant question is whether they functioned as a joint economic enterprise.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. All four Kerr v. Baranow JFV factors are present; the remedy is a proportionate share of accumulated wealth from the joint venture.
why_C_wrong: A JFV finding does not automatically produce a 50% split. The remedy is proportionate to Angela's actual contribution to the joint venture — which may be more or less than 50%, determined by the court on all the evidence.
why_D_wrong: A joint family venture is a judicially determined equitable concept, not a formal partnership. No written partnership agreement is required. The court assesses the substance of the relationship using the four factors.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Joint Family Venture — Four Kerr v. Baranow Factors
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269

tested_concepts: four JFV factors Kerr v Baranow
all four factors can be present with two earners
JFV not limited to one partner at home

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Presumption of Resulting Trust — Gratuitous Transfer Between Partners
fact_pattern: During their common-law relationship, Craig transferred $80,000 from his personal savings account to an investment account he registered solely in his partner Leigh's name, without any written explanation. Three years later, they separate. Craig claims the money was always his and that Leigh holds it on a resulting trust for him. Leigh argues it was a gift to her.
call_of_question: What is the presumption of resulting trust and how does it apply to Craig's $80,000 transfer to Leigh?

options.A: In a common-law relationship, any transfer of money between partners is presumed to be a gift; Craig bears the burden of proving otherwise.
options.B: Under the law of resulting trusts, where one person gratuitously transfers property to another person with whom they are not married, there is a presumption of resulting trust — the recipient holds the property on trust for the transferor; Craig does not need to prove the money was a loan; Leigh bears the burden of rebutting the presumption by proving Craig intended a gift.
options.C: The presumption of resulting trust applies only to transfers of real property, not to money transfers.
options.D: The presumption of resulting trust applies only between spouses, not between common-law partners.

explanation: The presumption of resulting trust arises at common law when a person gratuitously transfers property to another person who is not their spouse. In such cases, the law presumes the recipient holds the property on a resulting trust for the transferor — the transferor's intent is presumed to be to retain beneficial ownership. The recipient bears the burden of rebutting this presumption by showing the transferor intended an outright gift. Note that for married spouses, the presumption of advancement applies in the opposite direction (a gratuitous transfer between spouses is presumed a gift). Craig and Leigh are not married, so the resulting trust presumption applies to the $80,000 transfer; Leigh must prove Craig intended a gift to rebut it.
why_A_wrong: The presumption of advancement (gift) applies between spouses (married) or from parent to child. It does not automatically apply between common-law partners. For non-spousal, non-parent-child gratuitous transfers, the presumption of resulting trust applies.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The presumption of resulting trust applies to gratuitous transfers between non-spouses; Leigh bears the burden of proving donative intent to rebut the presumption.
why_C_wrong: The presumption of resulting trust applies to all types of gratuitous transfers — money, real property, personal property — not only to real estate transfers. The $80,000 cash transfer attracts the presumption.
why_D_wrong: The presumption of resulting trust applies between non-spouses, including common-law partners. It is the presumption of advancement (the opposite presumption, toward gift) that applies between married spouses. Common-law partners attract the resulting trust presumption for gratuitous transfers.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Presumption of Resulting Trust — Gratuitous Transfers
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Pecore v. Pecore, [2007] 1 SCR 795; Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269

tested_concepts: presumption of advancement for married spouses
recipient bears burden to prove gift
applies to all types of property

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Definition of Common-Law Spouse Under FLA
subtopic_heading: FLA s.29 Definition — Cohabited 3 Years Continuously or Parent of Child
fact_pattern: David and Isabella began cohabiting in a conjugal relationship in January 2021. Their son Lucas was born in March 2022. In August 2023 — after 2 years and 7 months of cohabitation — David moves out and the relationship ends. Isabella seeks spousal support. David argues she does not qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 because they cohabited for less than three years and therefore she has no support entitlement. Isabella argues she qualifies regardless of the cohabitation period.
call_of_question: Does Isabella qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 entitled to seek spousal support despite the cohabitation period being under three years?

options.A: No — FLA s.29 requires continuous cohabitation for at least three years; at 2 years and 7 months, Isabella falls short and does not qualify regardless of any other circumstances.
options.B: No — FLA s.29 requires five years of cohabitation for an unmarried couple; 2 years and 7 months is far below the threshold.
options.C: Yes — FLA s.29 defines 'spouse' for Part III support purposes as a person who has cohabited in a conjugal relationship continuously for at least three years OR who is in a relationship of some permanence and is the parent of a child born of the relationship; because Isabella and David are the parents of Lucas, Isabella qualifies under the parenthood arm of the disjunctive definition even though cohabitation was under three years.
options.D: Yes — any cohabitation in a conjugal relationship, regardless of duration or children, qualifies under FLA s.29.

explanation: Family Law Act s.29 defines 'spouse' for the purpose of Part III (support obligations) as a person who has cohabited with another person continuously for a period of not less than three years, OR who is in a relationship of some permanence if they are the natural or adoptive parents of a child. This is a disjunctive definition: either arm independently establishes qualification. Isabella and David cohabited for only 2 years and 7 months — below the three-year threshold under the first arm. However, they are the natural parents of Lucas, born during the relationship — satisfying the second arm. Isabella therefore qualifies as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 through the parenthood arm, despite falling short of three years' cohabitation. David's argument focuses only on the first arm and ignores the parenthood disjunct — a common exam trap.
why_A_wrong: General Rule Trap. David correctly identifies the three-year cohabitation threshold as the first arm of FLA s.29, but ignores the second (parenthood) arm. FLA s.29 is disjunctive — satisfying either arm is sufficient. Because Isabella and David are parents of Lucas, she qualifies under the parenthood arm even though cohabitation fell short of three years.
why_B_wrong: Wrong Timeline Trap. FLA s.29 requires three years of continuous cohabitation (not five) under the first arm. The five-year threshold does not appear in FLA s.29. Additionally, this answer again ignores the parenthood disjunct that independently qualifies Isabella.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.29 has a disjunctive definition: three years of continuous cohabitation OR parenthood of a child together in a relationship of some permanence; David and Isabella's parenthood of Lucas satisfies the second arm even though cohabitation was under three years.
why_D_wrong: Overstatement Trap. Not any cohabitation in a conjugal relationship qualifies under FLA s.29. The statute imposes a minimum — three continuous years or parenthood — to establish spousal status for support purposes. Very brief cohabitation without children does not meet the definition.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: FLA s.29 — Definition of Spouse for Support — Disjunctive Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29

tested_concepts: FLA s.29 disjunctive definition — three years OR parenthood
parenthood arm qualifies below three-year cohabitation threshold

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Domestic Violence Remedies for Unmarried Partners
subtopic_heading: Domestic Violence — Injunctive Relief and Protection Orders for Unmarried Partners
fact_pattern: Margaret and Eric have cohabited for 2 years without marrying. Eric has become increasingly violent toward Margaret. She wants to seek an order restraining Eric from the home and from having any contact with her. Eric argues that the exclusive possession and restraining order provisions under the FLA apply only to married spouses and Margaret cannot access these protections.
call_of_question: What legal remedies are available to Margaret as an unmarried common-law partner facing domestic violence?

options.A: Eric is correct; FLA exclusive possession and restraining orders are only available to married spouses; Margaret has no family law remedies and must rely solely on criminal law.
options.B: Unmarried cohabiting partners have access to protection orders and restraining orders under the FLA for persons who meet the definition of 'spouse'; for those who do not meet the threshold, the Family Law Act and the Domestic Violence Protection Act (DVPA) still provide mechanisms, and a restraining order under FLA s.46 is available to persons who have cohabited; a peace bond or emergency protection order may also be sought.
options.C: Margaret must first obtain a marriage certificate before she can access any family law protective orders.
options.D: Margaret can only access criminal law remedies (peace bond, assault charges); family law protection orders are unavailable to unmarried partners in Ontario.

explanation: Ontario provides multiple protective mechanisms for persons in intimate relationships who are at risk of domestic violence, regardless of marital status. The Family Law Act s.46 provides that a court may make an order restraining a person from molesting, annoying, or harassing the applicant or children in their lawful custody. Restraining orders under s.46 are available to persons who have cohabited even if they do not meet the three-year FLA s.29 spousal support threshold. Additionally, Ontario's Family Court may grant protective orders under the Children's Law Reform Act and the Family Law Rules. Criminal law remedies (peace bonds, no-contact conditions as bail conditions, assault charges) are also available. While the FLA exclusive possession regime (Part II) technically applies to 'spouses' (which for Part II purposes is limited to married spouses), other provisions and statutes provide meaningful protection for unmarried partners.
why_A_wrong: Unmarried cohabiting partners are not without family law remedies. FLA s.46 restraining orders, criminal peace bonds, and other protective mechanisms are available regardless of marital status. Eric's argument is incorrect.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.46 restraining orders are available to persons who have cohabited; peace bonds and other criminal remedies supplement these; married status is not required for all protective orders.
why_C_wrong: A marriage certificate is not required to access protective orders. FLA s.46 and criminal law remedies are available to cohabiting partners regardless of marital status.
why_D_wrong: Family law protection orders — specifically restraining orders under FLA s.46 — are available to unmarried cohabiting partners. Margaret is not limited to criminal law remedies only.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Domestic Violence — Remedies for Unmarried Partners
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 46; Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29

tested_concepts: FLA s.46 restraining order available to cohabiting partners
criminal and family law remedies concurrent

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Constructive Trust vs. FLA Equalization — No Equalization for Unmarried Partners
fact_pattern: Tom and Diane cohabited for 8 years. On separation, Tom's lawyer advises him that their property dispute will be resolved differently from a married couple's dispute. Specifically, the lawyer notes that the FLA equalization regime — which calculates each spouse's net family property and divides the difference — will not apply.
call_of_question: Why does the FLA net family property equalization scheme not apply to Tom and Diane's property dispute?

options.A: Because they did not register a domestic partnership agreement.
options.B: Because FLA Part I equalization of net family property applies exclusively to married spouses; since Tom and Diane were never married, neither may claim equalization, and their property dispute must be resolved through equitable doctrines such as unjust enrichment and constructive trust.
options.C: Because they did not cohabit for at least ten years.
options.D: Because they do not have children together, which is required before the FLA property regime applies.

explanation: Part I of the Family Law Act — which includes the equalization of net family property scheme — applies only to persons who are or were married to each other. The FLA s.1(1) definition of 'spouse' for Part I purposes excludes unmarried cohabiting partners. Since Tom and Diane were never married, neither the equalization entitlement nor the matrimonial home exclusive possession rights under Part II (which also uses a marital definition) are available to them. Their property dispute is governed by common law equitable principles — primarily unjust enrichment leading to constructive trust or a monetary remedy.
why_A_wrong: A domestic partnership agreement is not a prerequisite for FLA equalization. The eligibility is determined by marital status. Even with such an agreement, the FLA equalization scheme would not apply to an unmarried couple; such an agreement would instead govern their property division by its own contractual terms.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA Part I equalization is restricted to married spouses; Tom and Diane must rely on equitable doctrines (unjust enrichment, constructive trust) to resolve their property dispute.
why_C_wrong: There is no ten-year cohabitation threshold in the FLA that triggers equalization. The FLA Part I equalization scheme does not apply to common-law partners regardless of the length of their relationship.
why_D_wrong: Children together is one of the alternative thresholds for the FLA s.29 spousal support definition, not the FLA Part I property equalization scheme. Property equalization requires marriage, not parenthood.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: No FLA Equalization for Common-Law Partners
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 1(1), Part I; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834

tested_concepts: FLA Part I married spouses only

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Matrimonial Home — No Exclusive Possession for Unmarried Partners
fact_pattern: Paul and Nina cohabited for 6 years in a home registered solely in Paul's name. They separate and Nina, who has custody of their child, wants to stay in the home. Paul demands she leave immediately. Nina's lawyer considers whether to bring a motion for exclusive possession of the home under Part II of the Family Law Act.
call_of_question: Is a court order for exclusive possession of the home under FLA Part II available to Nina as an unmarried partner?

options.A: Yes — any cohabiting partner who has custody of a child may apply for exclusive possession under FLA Part II regardless of marital status.
options.B: Yes — FLA Part II exclusive possession applies to all persons who have cohabited for more than two years.
options.C: No — FLA Part II (matrimonial home and exclusive possession) applies only to married spouses; Nina and Paul are unmarried and Nina cannot access exclusive possession under Part II; she must seek relief through other means such as an injunction or occupancy order under different authority.
options.D: No — exclusive possession is only available to the registered owner of the home; since the home is in Paul's name, only Paul may seek exclusive possession.

explanation: Part II of the Family Law Act — which deals with the matrimonial home, including the right to exclusive possession — defines 'spouse' for Part II purposes to mean a married spouse. Section 17 of the FLA (exclusive possession) is a remedy available only to married spouses whose home qualifies as a 'matrimonial home' under the Act. Since Nina and Paul were never married, their shared residence does not constitute a 'matrimonial home' under the FLA and Nina cannot apply for exclusive possession under Part II. Nina may seek other remedies — such as an interlocutory injunction restraining Paul from interfering with her occupation, or relying on the registered ownership and equitable interest arguments — but FLA Part II exclusive possession is not available.
why_A_wrong: Having custody of a child does not extend FLA Part II exclusive possession rights to unmarried partners. Part II exclusive possession is available only to married spouses.
why_B_wrong: There is no two-year cohabitation threshold in the FLA that triggers Part II matrimonial home rights. FLA Part II applies to married spouses only, regardless of the length of any prior cohabitation.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA Part II matrimonial home exclusive possession is restricted to married spouses; Nina as an unmarried partner cannot use this provision and must pursue alternative legal remedies.
why_D_wrong: Exclusive possession under FLA Part II is not limited to the registered owner — indeed its purpose is often to grant the non-owner spouse exclusive possession over the titled spouse's objection. However, the entire Part II regime is unavailable to unmarried partners regardless of title.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Matrimonial Home — Unavailable to Unmarried Partners
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, Part II, s. 17 (exclusive possession)

tested_concepts: FLA Part II matrimonial home married spouses only
home not a matrimonial home for common-law

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Unjust Enrichment — Monetary Award vs. Constructive Trust Remedy
fact_pattern: After an 11-year common-law relationship, Brenda establishes unjust enrichment against her former partner Stan. The home — in Stan's name — has increased in value significantly, and Brenda's contributions (financial and domestic) were directly linked to maintaining and improving that property. Brenda seeks a constructive trust over the home rather than a monetary payment.
call_of_question: When is a constructive trust remedy preferred over a monetary award for unjust enrichment, and is Brenda entitled to seek it?

options.A: A constructive trust is always the preferred remedy for unjust enrichment between common-law partners; the court must impose it whenever unjust enrichment is established.
options.B: A monetary award is always preferred over a constructive trust in unjust enrichment cases because it avoids disrupting property title.
options.C: Under Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, a constructive trust is the appropriate remedy when there is a sufficiently substantial and direct link between the claimant's contributions and the disputed property; where the claimant's contributions are specifically tied to the acquisition, preservation, maintenance, or improvement of the property, a constructive trust may be imposed rather than merely a monetary award; Brenda's contributions directly linked to the home support the constructive trust remedy.
options.D: Constructive trust is only available where the parties had a written agreement specifying a property interest.

explanation: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court of Canada confirmed that the appropriate remedy for unjust enrichment may be either a monetary award or a constructive trust, depending on the connection between the claimant's contributions and the specific property. A constructive trust is appropriate where: (1) there is a sufficiently substantial and direct link between the contributions and the property; and (2) a monetary award would be inadequate (e.g., because the defendant is judgment-proof, or because the property is the very asset generated by the joint effort). Where, as here, Brenda's financial and domestic contributions directly maintained and improved the home — and the home has appreciated significantly — the court may impose a constructive trust giving Brenda a proportionate interest in the property rather than merely awarding a money judgment.
why_A_wrong: A constructive trust is not automatically imposed whenever unjust enrichment is established. The court has discretion and must assess whether a monetary remedy is adequate. A constructive trust requires a sufficiently direct link between the claimant's contributions and the specific property.
why_B_wrong: Monetary awards are not always preferred. Where the property has appreciated significantly due to the claimant's contributions, or where a monetary award would be inadequate to do justice, a constructive trust over the specific property is the appropriate remedy.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Kerr v. Baranow establishes that constructive trust is appropriate where there is a direct and substantial link between the claimant's contributions and the specific disputed property; Brenda's contributions satisfy this requirement.
why_D_wrong: A written agreement is not required to obtain a constructive trust remedy. Constructive trust arises from equitable principles to remedy unjust enrichment, not from contract. The court imposes it based on the parties' conduct and the nature of their contributions.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Constructive Trust vs. Monetary Award — Kerr v. Baranow
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834

tested_concepts: direct link to specific property required
not automatic upon unjust enrichment finding

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: SSAG Apply to Common-Law Support — Same Guidelines as Married Spouses
fact_pattern: Lisa and Dan cohabited for 7 years and meet the FLA s.29 definition of spouses. On separation, Lisa seeks spousal support from Dan. Dan's counsel argues that the Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG) apply only to married couples proceeding under the Divorce Act and cannot be used to calculate support for common-law partners under the FLA.
call_of_question: Do the Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG) apply to spousal support claims by common-law partners under the Family Law Act?

options.A: No — the SSAG are only applicable under the Divorce Act and cannot be used in FLA support proceedings for common-law partners.
options.B: Yes — the SSAG apply to spousal support claims under both the Divorce Act (for married spouses) and the Family Law Act (for qualifying common-law partners); courts routinely apply the SSAG ranges to FLA support claims and departing from SSAG ranges requires justification.
options.C: Yes — but for common-law partners the SSAG ranges are automatically reduced by 50% to account for the absence of formal marriage.
options.D: No — common-law partners must apply their own provincial SSAG formula developed exclusively for Ontario FLA claims.

explanation: The Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (SSAG) were designed to apply to spousal support claims under both the Divorce Act and provincial support legislation such as the Family Law Act. The SSAG preface and the case law confirm that the guidelines apply to qualifying common-law partners who meet the provincial 'spouse' definition with the same force as they apply to married spouses. Ontario courts regularly use SSAG formulas — both the 'without child support' and 'with child support' formulas — to determine the range of appropriate support amounts and duration for FLA claims. There is no discounting of the SSAG ranges for common-law status; the qualifying threshold of being a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 is what matters.
why_A_wrong: The SSAG expressly extend to claims under provincial support legislation including the FLA. Dan's argument is incorrect. The guidelines were developed for use beyond the Divorce Act context and are routinely applied in FLA proceedings.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The SSAG apply equally to FLA spousal support claims by qualifying common-law partners; courts treat SSAG ranges as the starting point in FLA support proceedings.
why_C_wrong: There is no automatic 50% reduction to SSAG ranges for common-law partners. Once a person qualifies as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29, the SSAG apply in full without a marital-status discount.
why_D_wrong: There is no separate Ontario-only provincial SSAG formula for FLA claims. The same national SSAG (developed by Rogerson and Thompson) apply across Canada to both DA and provincial support claims.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: SSAG Apply to Common-Law Spousal Support
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Spousal Support Advisory Guidelines (2008, updated 2016); Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29

tested_concepts: same guidelines for common-law and married

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: FLA s.29 — Parent of Child Alternative Threshold for Support
fact_pattern: Karen and Joseph cohabited for only 18 months in a conjugal relationship. They have one child together, born during the relationship. They separate and Karen seeks spousal support from Joseph under the Family Law Act. Joseph argues that Karen cannot qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 because their cohabitation was less than three years.
call_of_question: Does Karen qualify as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 for spousal support purposes?

options.A: No — Joseph is correct; FLA s.29 requires three continuous years of cohabitation regardless of whether they have children together; 18 months is insufficient.
options.B: Yes — Karen qualifies as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 because she and Joseph are the parents of a child together, which is an independent alternative threshold for the FLA s.29 definition of spouse; the three-year cohabitation threshold and the parent-of-a-child threshold are alternatives, not cumulative requirements.
options.C: No — the parent-of-a-child threshold only applies where the cohabitation has lasted at least two years, so 18 months still fails both thresholds.
options.D: Yes — any person who has a child with another person automatically qualifies as a 'spouse' under all provisions of the FLA.

explanation: Family Law Act s.29 defines 'spouse' for Part III (support) purposes to include: (a) a person who has cohabited with another person continuously for a period of not less than three years, OR (b) a person who is in a relationship of some permanence if they are the natural or adoptive parents of a child together. These two thresholds are alternatives — meeting either one suffices. Karen and Joseph have a child together. Even though they cohabited for only 18 months (below the three-year threshold), Karen meets the independent parent-of-a-child threshold. This is a common exam trap: candidates who remember only the three-year rule miss the alternative parental threshold.
why_A_wrong: Joseph is wrong. FLA s.29 provides two alternative thresholds: three years of continuous cohabitation OR being a parent of a child together. Karen meets the second threshold. Joseph's argument incorrectly treats the three-year requirement as the only path to support entitlement.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. FLA s.29 provides that parenthood of a child together is an independent alternative to the three-year cohabitation threshold; Karen qualifies as a 'spouse' for support purposes through the parental threshold.
why_C_wrong: There is no two-year cohabitation requirement linked to the parent-of-a-child threshold. The parent-of-a-child threshold under FLA s.29 has no minimum cohabitation period; it is met simply by being a natural or adoptive parent of a child together in a relationship of some permanence.
why_D_wrong: Parenthood does not automatically qualify a person as a 'spouse' under all FLA provisions. It is specifically the FLA s.29 Part III (support) definition that includes the parent-of-a-child threshold. FLA Part I (equalization) and Part II (matrimonial home) are not affected by parenthood.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: FLA s.29 — Parent of Child Alternative Threshold
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29

exam_trigger_words: less than three years but child
tested_concepts: three years not required if parent of child

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Unjust Enrichment — Monetary Award Calculation (Value Survived vs. Value Given)
fact_pattern: After 9 years together, Amara establishes unjust enrichment against her former partner Leon. The court must choose between two monetary remedy approaches: (1) awarding Amara the value of her contributions (quantum meruit — 'value given'); or (2) awarding her a proportionate share of the wealth Leon accumulated during the relationship ('value survived'). The home, a business, and investments represent Leon's accumulated wealth.
call_of_question: Under Kerr v. Baranow, what is the distinction between 'value given' and 'value survived' monetary remedies for unjust enrichment, and when does each apply?

options.A: 'Value given' and 'value survived' are synonymous terms that produce identical results in all cases.
options.B: Under Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269: 'value given' (quantum meruit) awards the claimant the objective value of her services rendered; 'value survived' awards a proportionate share of the wealth accumulated through the joint effort; where the parties operated as a joint family venture, 'value survived' is the appropriate measure and may produce a larger award; where no joint family venture existed, 'value given' is the measure.
options.C: 'Value survived' always produces a larger award and should always be used for common-law partners.
options.D: Ontario courts use only quantum meruit ('value given') for common-law property disputes.

explanation: In Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269, the Supreme Court distinguished between two approaches to calculating monetary remedies for unjust enrichment in the context of common-law relationships. 'Value given' (quantum meruit) calculates the objective market value of the claimant's services or contributions — essentially what it would have cost to hire someone to perform those services. 'Value survived' calculates a proportionate share of the net wealth that survived and was accumulated through the joint effort of both parties. Where the parties engaged in a joint family venture, 'value survived' is the more appropriate measure because it reflects the reality that their joint effort contributed to growing the pool of assets. Where no joint family venture is found, 'value given' remains the default measure.
why_A_wrong: 'Value given' and 'value survived' are distinct remedial approaches that can produce very different results — especially in long relationships where wealth has appreciated significantly. They are not synonymous and do not produce identical outcomes.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Kerr v. Baranow distinguishes 'value given' (quantum meruit for services) from 'value survived' (proportionate share of accumulated wealth via joint family venture); the latter applies where a JFV is established.
why_C_wrong: 'Value survived' does not always produce a larger award and is not always the correct measure. Where no joint family venture is found, or where the claimant's contributions were not integral to the wealth accumulation, 'value given' may be more appropriate — and may produce a higher or lower award than 'value survived' depending on the facts.
why_D_wrong: Ontario courts use both approaches. Kerr v. Baranow expressly authorizes and directs the 'value survived' approach where a joint family venture is established. Limiting all claims to quantum meruit was the approach rejected by the Supreme Court in that decision.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Unjust Enrichment Remedy — Value Given vs. Value Survived
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269

tested_concepts: value given vs value survived distinction
quantum meruit default where no JFV

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Unjust Enrichment — Domestic Labour as Enrichment: Pettkus v. Becker
fact_pattern: In a well-known fact pattern mirroring Pettkus v. Becker, Rosa contributed 19 years of domestic labour and farm work to a business registered solely in her partner Frank's name, receiving only a small allowance. On separation, Frank argues that Rosa's domestic contributions cannot ground an unjust enrichment claim because domestic services are not legally cognizable 'enrichments' — they are simply what partners do for each other and carry no market value in law.
call_of_question: Was Frank's argument accepted in Pettkus v. Becker, and what is the current law on domestic labour as enrichment?

options.A: Yes — the Supreme Court accepted that domestic labour cannot constitute an 'enrichment' because it carries no market value and is simply the expected contribution of a domestic partner.
options.B: No — in Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834, the Supreme Court of Canada rejected Frank's argument and held that domestic and farm labour contributions by a common-law partner can constitute an enrichment of the defendant, a corresponding deprivation of the claimant, and, in the absence of a juristic reason, ground an unjust enrichment claim; domestic services have cognizable market value and domestic labour is a legitimate basis for unjust enrichment.
options.C: Domestic labour can ground unjust enrichment but only if it has been performed for a minimum of ten years.
options.D: Domestic labour is not an enrichment but may qualify as a contribution toward a resulting trust if the labour can be quantified.

explanation: In Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834, the Supreme Court of Canada (per Dickson J.) held that Rosa's 19 years of domestic labour and farm work constituted an unjust enrichment of Frank entitling her to a constructive trust over a portion of his beekeeping business. The Court firmly rejected the argument that domestic labour lacks cognizable value in law. Domestic services — cooking, cleaning, farming, child-rearing — have a market equivalent and represent a real economic contribution. This decision was foundational in establishing that common-law partners can claim an interest in property through the unjust enrichment doctrine, specifically where domestic labour frees the other partner to accumulate wealth. Kerr v. Baranow (2011) confirmed and refined this framework.
why_A_wrong: The Supreme Court in Pettkus v. Becker rejected this argument entirely. Frank's position was not accepted. The Court held that domestic and farm labour by a cohabiting partner is a legally cognizable enrichment that can ground an unjust enrichment claim.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Pettkus v. Becker established that domestic labour constitutes a cognizable enrichment at law; the Supreme Court imposed a constructive trust in Rosa's favour based on her unpaid domestic and farm contributions.
why_C_wrong: There is no ten-year minimum for domestic labour to constitute enrichment. The duration of the relationship is relevant to the extent of the enrichment and the appropriate remedy, but there is no minimum threshold before domestic labour can ground an unjust enrichment claim.
why_D_wrong: Domestic labour grounds unjust enrichment directly — it need not be recharacterized as a contribution to a resulting trust. The resulting trust analysis applies primarily to gratuitous transfers of property or funds, not to labour contributions. Unjust enrichment is the applicable doctrine for domestic labour claims.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Pettkus v. Becker — Domestic Labour as Unjust Enrichment
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834; Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Presumption of Advancement — Reversed for Unmarried Partners vs. Married Spouses
fact_pattern: Arthur transfers a condominium unit into joint registration with his partner Vera during their 5-year common-law relationship, without any written agreement. When they separate, Vera claims she owns a 50% interest as a gift; Arthur claims the transfer was made for administrative convenience and he intended to retain the full beneficial interest. The question is which presumption applies.
call_of_question: What presumption applies to Arthur's gratuitous transfer of an interest in the condominium to Vera, and who bears the burden of rebutting it?

options.A: The presumption of advancement applies; Vera is presumed to have received a gift; Arthur must prove otherwise.
options.B: Because Arthur and Vera are not married, the presumption of resulting trust applies to the gratuitous transfer; the law presumes Arthur did not intend to gift the beneficial interest; Vera bears the burden of proving Arthur intended a gift, which may be rebutted by evidence of donative intent.
options.C: No presumption applies between common-law partners; the court decides on a balance of probabilities without any default presumption.
options.D: The presumption of advancement applies after two years of cohabitation, converting the resulting trust presumption.

explanation: The presumption of advancement (gift) applies between married spouses and from parent to child — where a gratuitous transfer is made between these parties, the law presumes it was intended as a gift and the transferor must rebut that presumption. For transfers between persons who are not in these relationships — including common-law partners — the presumption of resulting trust applies. As confirmed in Pecore v. Pecore, [2007] 1 SCR 795, the presumption of resulting trust means the recipient holds the transferred property on trust for the transferor; the recipient (Vera) must prove donative intent to keep the property. The length of cohabitation does not change the applicable presumption.
why_A_wrong: The presumption of advancement applies between married spouses, not common-law partners. Arthur and Vera are not married; accordingly, the presumption of resulting trust — not advancement — applies to this transfer.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The presumption of resulting trust applies to gratuitous transfers between non-married parties, including common-law partners; Vera as recipient bears the burden of proving Arthur intended a gift.
why_C_wrong: The law does not operate without a presumption in cases of gratuitous transfers. The presumption of resulting trust applies as the default for transfers between persons not in a marital or parent-child relationship. The burden is then on the recipient to rebut it.
why_D_wrong: There is no rule converting the presumption of resulting trust into the presumption of advancement after any period of cohabitation. The nature of the relationship (married vs. unmarried) at the time of transfer determines which presumption applies — not the duration of cohabitation.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Presumption of Resulting Trust — Common-Law vs. Married Spouses
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Pecore v. Pecore, [2007] 1 SCR 795; Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269

tested_concepts: recipient bears burden to prove gift

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: Resulting Trust vs. Constructive Trust — Distinguishing the Two Doctrines
fact_pattern: Two competing arguments are raised on behalf of common-law partners at separation. The first partner, Owen, argues that his contribution of $45,000 toward the purchase of a home registered in his partner Cynthia's name alone gives rise to a resulting trust — he paid and therefore retains a beneficial interest proportionate to his financial contribution. Cynthia argues that the better analysis is unjust enrichment and constructive trust, because over 10 years she contributed extensive domestic labour that equally benefited Owen.
call_of_question: What is the fundamental distinction between a resulting trust claim and a constructive trust (unjust enrichment) claim in this context?

options.A: A resulting trust and a constructive trust are the same remedy and produce identical outcomes in all cases.
options.B: A resulting trust arises from the express agreement of the parties to hold property on trust; a constructive trust arises from statute.
options.C: A resulting trust arises from a gratuitous transfer where the transferor retains the beneficial interest proportionate to the financial contribution made at the time of acquisition; it is based on the intent inferred at the moment of transfer; a constructive trust arises from the court's remedy for unjust enrichment, which may take into account a broader range of contributions (including labour) over the entire relationship to determine the proportionate interest.
options.D: A resulting trust requires proof of all three elements of unjust enrichment; a constructive trust does not.

explanation: A resulting trust and a constructive trust are distinct equitable doctrines. A resulting trust arises where property is transferred to one party who did not provide full consideration — the law presumes the beneficial interest results back to the transferor in proportion to their financial contribution at the time of acquisition. The focus is on the initial transfer and the financial contribution made at that moment. A constructive trust, by contrast, is the remedy imposed by a court to address unjust enrichment — it can take into account a broader range of contributions over the entire relationship (financial and non-financial, including domestic labour) and is not limited to the proportion of the initial purchase price. Owen's resulting trust claim is based solely on his $45,000 initial contribution; Cynthia's constructive trust argument encompasses the full scope of contributions over 10 years.
why_A_wrong: Resulting trust and constructive trust are distinct doctrines with different bases, different evidentiary requirements, and potentially different proportionate outcomes. They are not the same remedy and can produce significantly different results on the same facts.
why_B_wrong: A resulting trust does not require an express agreement — it arises by operation of law from the presumed intent at the time of transfer. A constructive trust arises from the court's equitable remedy for unjust enrichment, not from statute.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Resulting trust focuses on the financial contribution at time of acquisition and the proportionate beneficial interest; constructive trust is the court's remedy for unjust enrichment encompassing broader contributions over the whole relationship.
why_D_wrong: This has the analysis backward. Unjust enrichment requires proof of three elements (enrichment, deprivation, no juristic reason), and constructive trust is one of the remedies for unjust enrichment. Resulting trust does not require proof of the unjust enrichment elements; it arises from the transfer itself.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: Resulting Trust vs. Constructive Trust — Distinct Doctrines
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Kerr v. Baranow, [2011] 1 SCR 269; Pecore v. Pecore, [2007] 1 SCR 795; Pettkus v. Becker, [1980] 2 SCR 834

tested_concepts: resulting trust based on initial financial contribution
constructive trust remedy for unjust enrichment

subject: family_law
topic_heading: Definition of Common-Law Spouse Under FLA
subtopic_heading: Cohabitation — Continuous vs. Interrupted; What Breaks the Chain
fact_pattern: Sam and Laura began cohabiting in a conjugal relationship in January 2020. In July 2021, they separated for 4 months during which they lived apart and had no intimate relationship. In November 2021, they reconciled and resumed cohabiting. They finally and permanently separate in March 2024. Laura now seeks spousal support under FLA s.29. Sam argues that the four-month gap in 2021 broke the continuity of cohabitation, resetting the clock, so they did not cohabit 'continuously' for three years.
call_of_question: Does the four-month separation in 2021 break the continuity of cohabitation for FLA s.29 purposes, preventing Laura from meeting the three-year threshold?

options.A: Yes — any interruption in cohabitation, however brief, completely resets the three-year clock under FLA s.29.
options.B: Yes — an interruption of more than 90 days always resets the cohabitation clock under FLA s.29.
options.C: No — a temporary separation that does not constitute a permanent breakdown of the relationship does not necessarily break the continuity of cohabitation for FLA s.29 purposes; courts assess the overall nature of the relationship, and a temporary reconcilable separation may be treated as a period within a continuous conjugal relationship; the total period of cohabitation may still satisfy the three-year threshold.
options.D: No — FLA s.29 contains an express provision that periods of separation of less than 6 months are disregarded for continuity purposes.

explanation: FLA s.29 requires cohabitation 'continuously' for a period of not less than three years in a conjugal relationship. Courts have interpreted 'continuously' to not necessarily require unbroken day-by-day cohabitation. A temporary separation that does not amount to a permanent breakdown of the relationship — where the parties intended to and did resume cohabitation — may be characterized as a period within an ongoing conjugal relationship. Courts look at the totality of the relationship to determine whether the overall character of the relationship was one of continuous conjugal cohabitation. Sam and Laura's four-month gap that ended in reconciliation and further cohabitation until March 2024 will be assessed contextually, and is unlikely to be treated as a permanent disruption resetting the three-year clock. Note that option D incorrectly states a statutory provision that does not exist.
why_A_wrong: A brief interruption does not automatically reset the continuity clock. Courts assess whether the separation was temporary (and the relationship continued) or permanent (ending the conjugal relationship). A four-month gap followed by reconciliation and years of resumed cohabitation would not automatically disqualify the claim.
why_B_wrong: There is no 90-day rule in the FLA resetting the cohabitation clock. This is a fabricated threshold. The FLA does not specify any particular duration of interruption that categorically breaks continuity.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Courts assess the overall nature of the relationship; a temporary reconcilable separation does not necessarily break continuity for FLA s.29 purposes, and the parties' total cohabitation may still satisfy the three-year threshold.
why_D_wrong: Option D correctly states the outcome but wrongly attributes it to an express statutory provision that does not exist in the FLA. There is no express 6-month rule in the FLA. The result flows from judicial interpretation of 'continuously' rather than from an express statutory exclusion.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: FLA s.29 — Continuous Cohabitation and Temporary Separation
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: FLA s.29 Support — Entitlement Grounds Same as Married Spouses
fact_pattern: After 4 years of cohabitation, Michelle qualifies as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29 and applies for spousal support. The court must determine which support entitlement grounds apply. Dan's counsel argues that common-law spousal support is limited to economic hardship caused by the separation and cannot be based on compensatory grounds (i.e., compensation for career sacrifices or role-merger during the relationship).
call_of_question: What entitlement grounds apply to a qualifying common-law spousal support claim under the FLA, and is compensatory entitlement available?

options.A: Common-law spousal support under the FLA is limited to needs-based (non-compensatory) grounds only; compensatory entitlement is available only under the Divorce Act.
options.B: FLA spousal support entitlement for qualifying common-law partners is assessed on the same grounds as for married spouses — compensatory, non-compensatory (needs-based), and contractual entitlement grounds all apply; a qualifying common-law spouse who sacrificed career advancement to support the family relationship may establish compensatory entitlement just as a married spouse would.
options.C: Common-law spousal support under the FLA is always compensatory only; needs-based entitlement is not available.
options.D: The FLA provides no independent entitlement analysis; courts simply apply SSAG without first determining entitlement for common-law partners.

explanation: Once a person qualifies as a 'spouse' under FLA s.29, their spousal support claim is assessed under Part III of the FLA on the same substantive basis as a married spouse's claim. The FLA s.33 support obligation and the factors and objectives in ss.33(8) and (9) apply fully to qualifying common-law spouses. Entitlement may be established on compensatory grounds (where the applicant suffered economic disadvantage from the relationship, such as career sacrifice to raise children or support the other's career), non-compensatory grounds (where the applicant has needs and the respondent has the means to contribute), or contractual grounds. Dan's counsel's argument that compensatory entitlement is only available under the Divorce Act is incorrect.
why_A_wrong: FLA Part III support provisions apply compensatory, non-compensatory, and contractual entitlement grounds to qualifying common-law partners the same as to married spouses. Compensatory entitlement is not restricted to the Divorce Act.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Qualifying common-law spouses under FLA s.29 have access to all three entitlement grounds — compensatory, non-compensatory, and contractual — on the same basis as married spouses under the FLA.
why_C_wrong: Non-compensatory (needs-based) entitlement is available to qualifying common-law partners under the FLA, not only compensatory entitlement. All three grounds are available.
why_D_wrong: Courts must first determine entitlement before proceeding to quantum. The SSAG are used to calculate the quantum and duration of support, but entitlement is a threshold analysis that applies to both married and common-law support claims under the FLA.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: FLA Spousal Support — Entitlement Grounds for Common-Law Partners
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, ss. 29, 33(8), 33(9)

tested_concepts: FLA Part III entitlement grounds apply to common-law
same substantive test as married spouses

subject: family_law
subtopic_heading: FLA s.29 Support — Limitation Period (Two-Year Limitation Under Limitations Act 2002)
fact_pattern: Patrick and Gina cohabited for 5 years and separated in January 2021. Gina qualifies as a spouse under FLA s.29. She did not seek spousal support at the time of separation. In March 2024 — over three years after separation — Gina consults a lawyer about bringing a claim for spousal support under the FLA. Patrick's lawyer raises a limitation period defence.
call_of_question: Is Gina's FLA spousal support claim statute-barred, and what limitation period applies to an initial spousal support application under the FLA?

options.A: There is no limitation period on spousal support claims; Gina may apply at any time after separation.
options.B: The limitation period for a spousal support application under the FLA is six years from separation.
options.C: The basic limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 (Ontario) is two years from the date the claim was discovered; for an initial spousal support application under the FLA, the two-year limitation period applies and begins to run when the applicant knew or ought to have known of the claim; Gina's 2024 application is more than two years after her January 2021 separation, and Patrick's limitation defence is potentially valid unless Gina can show the claim was not discoverable until within two years of her application.
options.D: Spousal support claims under the FLA are subject to a one-year limitation period from the date of separation.

explanation: Unlike child support — which has no limitation period under the Divorce Act or the FCSG — initial spousal support claims under the Family Law Act are subject to the general limitation period under Ontario's Limitations Act, 2002. The basic limitation period is two years from the date the claim was discovered (s. 4 Limitations Act, 2002). For a spousal support claim, the limitation period typically runs from the date of separation (when the claim is first discoverable). Gina separated in January 2021 and did not apply until March 2024 — over three years later. Patrick's limitation defence has merit unless Gina can establish that the claim was not discoverable until within two years before her application (e.g., due to incapacity, non-disclosure, or other recognized grounds for postponement of the limitation period).
why_A_wrong: Initial spousal support applications under the FLA are subject to the two-year basic limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002. There is no blanket exemption from limitation periods for spousal support. (Note: ongoing support obligations once an order exists are treated differently from initial applications.)
why_B_wrong: The limitation period is two years under the Limitations Act, 2002 — not six years. Ontario moved from a six-year limitation period to a two-year basic limitation period when the Limitations Act, 2002 came into force.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The two-year limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002 applies to initial FLA spousal support applications from the date of discovery (typically separation); Gina's three-year delay makes Patrick's limitation defence potentially valid.
why_D_wrong: There is no one-year limitation period for spousal support claims under the FLA. The Limitations Act, 2002 provides a two-year basic limitation period. A one-year rule is not found in Ontario's family law or limitations legislation.

source_reference.subject: Family Law
source_reference.chapter: Chapter 13 — Common-Law Partners and Property Rights
source_reference.heading: FLA Spousal Support — Limitation Period Under Limitations Act 2002
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Family Law Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. F.3, s. 29; Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4

tested_concepts: two-year limitation period FLA spousal support
Limitations Act 2002 applies to FLA claims
not six years not one year
